source_metadata.title_raw: Das Williams - A-18-102 - June 26, 2018 - Santa Barbara

content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
1102 Q StTeet • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811 -
(91 6) 32 2·-5660 • Fax ( 91 6) 322-0886 
Das Williams 
County Supervisor 1st District 
County of Santa Barbara 
105 E. Anapamu Street, 4th Floor 
Santa Barbara, CA 93101 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-18-102 
Dear Mr. Williams: 
June 26, 2018 
This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict of interest provisions of 
the Political Reform Act (the "Act")1 and Section 1090, not under other general conflict of interest 
prohibitions such as common law conflict of interest.2 
Also, note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 
FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is 
not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for 
additional advice. 
In regard to our advice on Section 1090, we are required to forward your request and all 
pertinent facts relating to the request to theAttomey General's Office and the Santa Barbara County 
District Attorney's Office, which we have done. (Section l097. l(c)(3).) We did not receive a 
written response from either entity. (Section 1097.l(c)(4).) We are also required to advise you that, 
for purposes of Section 1090, the following advice "is not admissible in a criminal proceeding 
against any individual other than the requestor." (See Section 1097.l(c)(5).) 
QUESTIONS 
1. Under the Act, may you participate in decisions before the SBCAG or the Santa Barbara Board 
of Supervisors involving the South Coast HOV Project when you own real property located 
within 500 feet of an onramp to Highway 101 that provides access to a portion of the highway 
that is in Phase 4, Segment 4A of the project? 
· 
1 The Political Refonn Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 
2 This advice letter is a follow up to the Ghizzoni Advice Letter, No. A-17-250 and incorporates additional 
facts provided. 


File No. A-18-102 
Page No. 2 
2. Under Section 1090 may you participate in making any decisions before SBCAG involving 
ongoing litigation or settlement agreements related to the South Coast HOV project~ 
CONCLUSIONS 
1. Yes. You may participate in decisions before the SBCAG or the Santa Barbara Board of 
Supervisors involving the South Coast HOV Project because there is no reasonably 
foreseeable material financial effect upon your interests. 
2. While you may be prohibited from taking part in decisions regarding the litigation under the 
Act if the litigation affects Segment 4A, Section 1090 does not apply to you for purposes of 
litigation involving the South Coast HOV project. 
FACTS AS PROVIDED BY REQUESTOR 
You are a Santa Barbara County First District Supervisor. The residence you own with your 
wife is on 0.15 acres ofland in the City of Carpinteria, California. The South Coast HOV.Project 
("101 HOV Project") proposes to add one high occupancy vehicle lane in each direction from south 
of the City of Carpinteria to the City of Santa Barbara. This will result in a six-lane stretch of 
freeway for that stretch of highway. The 101 HOV Project Phase 4 is 10.9 miles in length. Your 
property line is 163 feet from the nearest onramp to Highway JOI and approximately 130 feet from 
the edge of Caltrans right of way for Highway 101. Caltrans is the lead agency for the 101 HOV 
Project. In your capacity as Supervisor you also serve on the board of SBCAG. The County of 
Santa Barbara and the Santa Barbara County Association of Governments (SBCAG) are project 
partners with Caltrans, and the County is a permitting authority for a portion of the 101 HOV 
Project. 
The 10 l HOV Project is divided into four phases. Phase 1, which stretches from Mil pas 
Street exit to Hot Springs exit, was already constructed in 2012. Phase 2, which stretches from 
Mussel Shoals to Carpinteria, was already constructed in 2015. Phase 3, which stretches from 
Linden Pass to Casitas Pass in Carpinteria, is currently being constructed. Phase 4, which stretches 
from Carpinteria to Santa Barbara, is not fully permitted or funded yet. There are five individual 
segments of Phase 4, Segment 4A, Segment 4B, Segment 4C, Segment 4D, and Segment 4E. 
Segment 4A is the segment closest to your residence, as noted above your property line is within 
163 feet of the nearest onramp to this portion of the highway and within 130 feet of the Caltrans 
right away. You have recused yourself from SBCAG decisions related to Segment 4A and are . 
seeking advice in your capacity as a member of SBCAG and as First District Supervisor on 
participation in the project with the exception of decisions effecting Segment 4A. 
Each segment of Phase 4 will be completed in a different fiscal year and each segment is 
subject to different permitting authorities.3 
3 Segment 4A- Ballard Avenue to Carpinteria City Limit: starts construction in FY 2020-21, permitting 
authority is the City of Carpinteria. Segment 4B - Carpinteria to Padaro Lane North: starts construction FY 2020-2021, 
permitting authority is the County of Santa Barbara. Segment 4D - Sheffield Interchange to Olive Mill Road: starts 
construction in FY 2023-2024, permitting authority is the County of Santa Barbara. Segment 4E - Olive Mill Road to 
Sycamore Creek: starts construction in FY 2023-2024, permitting authority is the City of Santa Barbara. 


. 
File No. A-18-102 
Page No. 3 
Segment 4A is going to be constructed first and will begin to be built before the other 
segments are all approved. Segment 4A's construction is not dependent on the other segments being 
approved. However, the rest of the segments are dependent upon other segments being approved. In 
other words, the construction of Segments 4B, 4C, 4D, an 4E are dependent on Segment 4A's 
construction, but Segment 4A is not dependent on Segments 4B, 4C, 4D, or 4E. 
Over the course of the remainder of the 101 HOV Project, the SBCAG Board will be 
making decisions regarding potential funding commitments, shared work and responsibilities with 
Caltrans, and construction. SBCAG is a significant funding source for the 101 HOV Project, 
planning to spend approximately $140 million in "Measure A" funding to support the project as a 
whole. Thus far, SBCAG has contributed approximately $10.87 million of the total 54.62 million 
required to construct Phase 4 of the 101 HOV Project. Caltrans will need a Coastal Development 
Permit from the County of Santa Barbara for a portion of Phase 4, but not for Segment 4A. 
Although not the initial decisionmaker on the permit, the Coastal Development Permit would be 
appealable to the Board of Supervisors. 
Caltrans prepared an Environmental Impact Report for the 101 HOV Project, Phase 4. In 
2014, two lawsuits were brought against Cal trans as Respondent and SBCAG and the County as 
Real Parties in Interest. (Grassini, et al. v. California Department of Transportation, et al. (Case 
No, 1468969) and Transportation Futures Comn:,ittee, et al. v. California Department of 
Transportation, et al. (Case No. 1468969).) 
In 2014, SBCAG remained in the ongoing litigation as the Real Party in Interest. In 2014, 
the parties entered into a stipulation to dismiss the County from the litigation and on November 17, 
2015, Grassini's Petition for Writ of Mandate was denied. That case is now concluded. On 
December 17, 2015, Transportation Futures Committee's Petition for Writ of Mandate was granted 
in part and denied in part. As a result of this decision, Caltrans revised the Environmental Impact 
Report and has released the document for public comment. The Transportation Futures litigation is 
ongomg. 
ANALYSIS 
The Act 
Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or using 
his or her position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial interest. 
A conflict of interest may arise only when it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a 
material financial effect on the official or his or her immediate family that is distinguishable from 
its effect on the public generally. (Section 87103.) 
At issue here is your interest in residential real property.4 For an interest that is not explicitly 
involved in a decision (such as your home), Regulation 18701(b) states that a financial effect can be 
reasonably foreseeable where it is a realistic possibility under ordinary circumstances. 
4 Under the Act, any real property in which the public official has a direct or indirect interest of at least $2,000, 
including a leasehold interest (other than a term of one month or less) held directly by an official or an official's spouse 


FileNo. A-18-102 
Page No. 4 
Regulation 18702.2(a) sets the materiality standard for real property interests and provides a 
list of circumstances to consider. As relevant to your facts, the financial effect will be material if the 
decisions: 
"(10) Would change the character of the parcel ofreal property by substantially 
altering traffic levels or intensity of use, including parking, of property surrounding 
the official's real property parcel, the view, privacy, noise levels, or air quality, 
including odors, or any other factors that would affect the market value of the real 
property parcel in which the official has a financial interest; 
"(12) Would cause a reasonably prudent person, using due care and consideration 
under the circumstances, to believe that the governmental decision was of such a 
nature that its reasonably foreseeable effect would influence the market value of the 
official's property." (Regulation 18702.2(a).) 
In this case, a new high occupancy lane will be added in each direction on Highway 101. 
Segment 4A will be the first segment built in Phase 4 and its construction is not dependent upon 
any other segment approval. In other words, the decisions to move forward on Segments 4B-E will 
not impact the plans to construct Segment 4A. It follows then that decisions concerning those 
remaining segments will not change the character of your parcel of real property by substantially 
altering the traffic levels or intensity of use surrounding your property as those segments are not 
surrounding your real property parcel. Additionally, a reasonably prudent person, using care and 
consideration under the circumstances would not believe that decisions related to funding, 
necessary permits and other decisions necessary to move Segments 4B-E forward would have an 
effect on the market value of your property. Therefore, you do not have a conflict of interest when 
participating in decisions related to Segments 4B-E. 
You have also mentioned that there is ongoing litigation involving the 101 HOV Project. 
You have not provided any details as to what the litigation decisions may entail. So long as these 
decisions would not affect Segment 4A, you are not prohibited from participating in such decisions. 
Section 1090 
Section 1090 generally prohibits public officers, while acting in their official capacities, 
from making contracts in which they are financially interested. Section 1090 is concerned with 
financial interests, other than remote or minimal interests, that prevent public officials from 
exercising absolute loyalty and undivided allegiance in furthering the best interests of their 
agencies. (Stigall v. Taft (1962) 58 Cal.2d 565, 569.) Section 1090 is intended "not only to strike at 
actual impropriety, but also to strike at the appearance of impropriety." ( City of Imperial Beach v. 
Bailey (1980) 103 Cal.App.3d 191, 197.) 
Under Section 1090, "the prohibited act is the making of a contract in which the official has 
a financial interest." (People v. Honig (1996) 48 Cal.App.4th 289, 333.) A contract that violates 
or by any business entity in which the official or the official's spouse own an interest of l O percent or more can give 
rise to a potential conflict. (Section 87103(b).) 


File No. A-18-102 
Page No. 5 
Section 1090 is void. (Thomson v. Call (1985) 38 Cal.3d 633, 646.) The prohibition applies 
regardless of whether the terms of the contract are fair and equitable to all parties. (Id. at pp. 646-
649.) 
Here, the decisions at issue involve ongoing litigation that SBCAG or the County is 
involved with. As a rule, an agreement to settle a lawsuit is treated as a contract. (See, e.g., T M 
Cobb Co. v. Superior Court (1984) 36 Cal.3d 273, 280; Weddington Productions, Inc. v. Flick, 
(1998) 60 Cal.App.4th 793, 810.) However, the pertinent issue is whether you have a prohibitory 
financial interest in the litigation involving the 101 HOV project for purposes of Section 1090. 
Previously, for purposes of a highway project, we have found that an official does not have 
an interest in a contract involving the highway project under Section 1090 merely because the 
official has a property or a business adjacent to or in close proximity to the project. (Bordsen 
Advice Letter, No. A-17-059.) Likewise, here, the close proximity of your real property to the 
highway would not alone establish a financial interest under Section 1090 with respect to any 
contract resulting from the litigation. Barring additional facts to the contrary, Section 1090 does not 
prohibit you from participating in decisions concerning settlement agreements in the ongoing 
litigation. 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660 . 
SKB:jgl 
Sincerely, 
Brian G. Lau 
. 
Assistant General Counsel 
By: 
Sukhi K. Brar 
Senior Counsel, Legal Division

Under the Act, may you participate in decisions before the SBCAG or the Santa Barbara Board of Supervisors involving the South Coast HOV Project when you own real property located within 500 feet of an onramp to Highway 101 that provides access to a portion of the highway that is in Phase 4, Segment 4A of the project? · 1 The Political Refonn Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 2 This advice letter is a follow up to the Ghizzoni Advice Letter, No. A-17-250 and incorporates additional facts provided. File No. A-18-102 Page No. 2 2. Under Section 1090 may you participate in making any decisions before SBCAG involving ongoing litigation or settlement agreements related to the South Coast HOV project~ CONCLUSIONS 1. Yes. You may participate in decisions before the SBCAG or the Santa Barbara Board of Supervisors involving the South Coast HOV Project because there is no reasonably foreseeable material financial effect upon your interests. 2. While you may be prohibited from taking part in decisions regarding the litigation under the Act if the litigation affects Segment 4A, Section 1090 does not apply to you for purposes of litigation involving the South Coast HOV project.
